text.title: MoSCoW Framework: Categories and Rules
text.summary: Defines the four MoSCoW categories, the fail-test for Must Have, and the 60/20/20 effort allocation rule.

rule.statement: Must Have: items that, if absent, cause the product to fail completely. Apply the fail-test verbatim — "if we don't have this, does the product work?" A "yes" means it is not Must.
rule.rationale: Without a binary test, teams default to calling everything Must. The fail-test forces explicit articulation of the viability condition per item.

rule.statement: Should Have: important but not critical. A reasonable workaround exists. Users can accomplish their goals without it, but the experience degrades noticeably.
rule.rationale: The workaround test distinguishes Should from Must. If the answer is "users can still proceed," it is Should.

rule.statement: Could Have: nice-to-have enhancements with low cost. First category to cut when Must+Should exceed capacity. Absence does not affect core functionality.
rule.rationale: Explicit "first to cut" designation protects Must and Should from false trade-offs when capacity is tight.

rule.statement: Won't Have: explicitly excluded from this release. Document every Won't item with a one-line reason and optionally a future-release target.
rule.rationale: Won't Have is the framework's primary scope defense. Undocumented exclusions re-enter as scope creep. The discomfort of writing Won't is the value of the exercise.

rule.statement: Must Have must target 60% of capacity. Must + Should must not exceed 80%. The remaining 20% absorbs Could items or overflow.
rule.rationale: Empirical heuristic from DSDM. If Must alone exceeds 60%, the release is over-scoped before a single Should item is considered.

rule.statement: Always require a capacity number before running MoSCoW. Without a timebox (eng-weeks, person-days, or sprint budget), categories are meaningless — you are producing a wish list, not a prioritization.
rule.rationale: MoSCoW categories are release-scoped, not absolute. The same feature can be Must in one release and Won't in the next depending on capacity and goal.

rule.statement: Force at least one item into Won't Have per session. If every item passes the fail-test, the fail-test is being applied too loosely — reapply with a contrarian perspective.
rule.rationale: Must inflation is the universal failure mode. A contrarian re-application ("a competitor ships without this and wins — still Must?") surfaces weak Musts.

example.note: Invoice app MVP: "User authentication" passes fail-test — the product cannot work at all without identity. Must Have.
"Password reset" is often called Must, but users can use support as a workaround. Should Have is correct unless compliance requires it.
